Design Thinking · Define Phase · Mahindra University

Impact of Power Loom Products on Authentic Handloom Pochampally Ikat Artisans

Unravelling the threads of a dying craft — and reimagining its future

Team
Idea Igniters
Institution
Mahindra University
School of Engineering
Academic Year
2025–26
Faculty Mentor
Prof. Sayoni
Section 01

User / Protagonist

🧵
Pochampally Handloom Weaver
Traditional Ikat Artisan
Age GroupMiddle-aged & above
LocationPochampally Village, Telangana
Primary RoleHandloom Ikat Weaving
Stall LocationsShilparamam & local markets
GI Tag ProductPochampally Ikat (since 2004)

Background & Story

The Pochampally weaving community is predominantly composed of middle-aged weavers and above, with minimal involvement of younger generations. This demographic pattern reflects a troubling generational gap driven by lack of interest and awareness of the economic consequences that await those who abandon the craft.

The key role of these weavers is to create and preserve traditional, handwoven Pochampally Ikat products that treasure Indian culture and heritage. Despite facing severe hardships — a competitive market disrupted by machine-made fabrics, falling incomes, and dwindling demand — these artisans remain steadfast, determined, and deeply passionate about keeping this centuries-old art alive.

Our Field Interaction

We consulted 2 local weavers operating stalls at Shilparamam, Hyderabad, to understand the depth and intensity of the issue from their perspective. These conversations revealed not only the financial burden they carry but also the deep emotional investment they have in their craft — one that machines simply cannot replicate.

Skills & Expertise

Resist Dyeing (Ikat) Warp Ikat Weft Ikat Double Ikat Pattern Design Natural Dyes Pit Loom Weaving

Artisan Voice

"Our craft takes days of painstaking work — tying, dyeing, aligning patterns — but customers walk away for a machine copy that costs a fraction of the price. How do we justify 20 days of our lives?"

Section 02

Context & Situation

The Machine Woven Era

The rise of machine-woven fabrics — employing highly advanced, efficient technologies across various stages of weaving — has directly led to a decline in handloom cloth sales. Power looms can simplify the weaving process, rapidly generate Pochampally-style fabrics, and duplicate printed Ikat designs with high-tech equipment that drastically reduces labour intensity.

Economic Displacement

Power looms lure customers away with significantly lower prices, creating unemployment and threatening the livelihoods of handloom weavers. The labour employed across various stages of the handloom process — not just the primary weavers but also dyers, warpers, and helpers — are all severely impacted by this structural shift in the textile economy.

The Systemic Failure

Government subsidies and cooperative societies exist but often fail due to bureaucratic delays, poor marketing, and limited digital outreach. Attempts to modernise production lack emotional and cultural sensitivity. Machines cannot replicate the soul of handwoven art — yet the market continues to reward quantity over quality, eroding the value of authentic craftsmanship.

📍 Shilparamam — Handloom Ikat sarees at an artisan's stall
📍 Field visit — Swathi SHG, Yadadri Bhuvanagiri · Pochampally Ikat Fabrics & Apparels
Section 03

Evidence Base

🗣️ In-depth Interviews — 2 Artisans (Shilparamam)
📋 Community Survey — Cross-age groups, varied backgrounds
👁️ Field Observations — Artisan stalls & markets
📖 Secondary Research — Academic literature & GI documentation
↓ Sales
Declining Handloom Demand

Survey data confirmed a widespread decline in handloom cloth sales as consumers increasingly choose cheaper machine-made alternatives that visually mimic authentic Ikat.

0%
Zero Digital Marketing Capability

Both artisans interviewed reported complete inability to engage with digital marketing, advertising, or premium branding across social media and e-commerce platforms.

GI Gap
Authenticity Cannot Be Justified

Despite the GI tag (awarded 2004), weavers lack the tools and skills to justify product authenticity and corresponding premium pricing at the point of sale.

High
Financial Burden is Severe

Artisans struggle to pay rent, support family requirements, fulfill financial obligations, and manage GST — all while incomes shrink due to power loom competition.

↑ Age
Youth Disengagement from the Craft

Younger generations are disengaging from weaving due to poor financial prospects and the social undervaluation of artisanal work — threatening generational continuity.

📸 Team Idea Igniters conducting field interviews with an artisan at Shilparamam, Hyderabad
Section 04

Pain Points & Needs

01
💔

Craft Overshadowed by Imitation

Authentic Pochampally Ikat is overshadowed by mass-produced imitations. The artistry that once symbolized cultural pride now competes with machine-made fabrics at a fraction of the price — leaving artisans feeling invisible and undervalued.

02
📉

Declining Demand & Shrinking Margins

Weavers face declining demand and shrinking profit margins as power loom goods dominate the market. The erosion of their creative identity and economic base is accelerating with no adequate support structure in place.

03
🌐

No IP Protection or Market Recognition

Weavers need fair market recognition and stronger intellectual property protection. Despite the GI tag, enforcement is weak and consumers at the point of sale have no reliable way to identify or value authentic handloom products.

04
😞

Emotional Exhaustion & Anxiety

Weavers experience deep frustration, helplessness, and anxiety. Watching their heritage diluted by imitation leaves them feeling invisible. Many report emotional exhaustion from constant bargaining and the fear of losing generational continuity in their craft.

05
🚫

Missing Support Systems

Support systems for marketing, design innovation, sustainable income, and digital access are urgently missing. Artisans lack modern tools that could enhance — not replace — their craftsmanship, leaving them structurally disadvantaged.

Section 05

Root Cause Analysis — 5 Whys

Problem: Handloom Pochampally Ikat artisans are experiencing a rapid decline in income and market share.
W1
Why are artisans losing income and market share?

Because power looms offer cheaper, faster alternatives that appeal to cost-conscious consumers — creating a systemic imbalance that traditional artisans cannot match on price or speed.

W2
Why can't artisans compete on these terms?

Because the market rewards quantity over quality, and handloom weavers lack technological integration, capital access, and weak policy enforcement for craft protection — structural inequities that have built up over decades.

W3
Why does the market fail to recognise and price authenticity?

Because consumers at the point of sale cannot distinguish authentic handloom Ikat from power loom imitations — the GI tag system exists but has not been implemented in a visible, consumer-accessible way.

W4
Why hasn't the GI system translated into consumer-facing protection?

Because cultural and educational gaps persist — young generations disengage from weaving, marketing literacy is near zero among artisans, and no accessible, low-cost tools exist to bridge the gap between artisan identity and consumer knowledge.

W5
Root Cause — Why do these gaps persist structurally?

Because policy frameworks, cooperative structures, and support systems are designed for administrative scale and bureaucratic compliance — not around the actual daily constraints of rural handloom weavers. The system rewards industrial efficiency, not artisanal authenticity, and no market infrastructure exists to tell the weaver's story where it matters most: at the point of purchase.

Section 06

Impact & Scale

Hundreds
Families in Pochampally Dependent on Weaving as Primary Livelihood
↓ Many
Families Now Abandoning the Craft Due to Unsustainable Incomes
GI 2004
Year of GI Tag Award — Yet Enforcement & Consumer Awareness Remain Low
High
Frequency of Market Losses & Emotional Distress Reported by Artisans
0
Effective Digital Marketing Tools Available to Artisans Today
At Risk
Centuries-Old Art Form & Telangana Heritage Identity
Critical Warning: If left unresolved, the Pochampally region risks losing its cultural legacy, artisanal skills, and economic stability. The extinction of authentic Ikat weaving would mean the disappearance of a centuries-old art form that defines Telangana's heritage — and the ripple effects on community identity and local economy would be irreversible. Government subsidies and cooperative societies exist but often fail due to bureaucratic delays, poor marketing, and limited digital outreach.
Section 07

Point of View Statement

Stanford d.school Define Phase · [User] needs [Need] because [Insight]
The traditional Pochampally handloom weaver — a skilled artisan preserving generations of cultural heritage and craftsmanship —  needs to  sustain their livelihood and creative identity by finding equitable ways to compete with mechanized power loom production  because  mass-produced imitations have devalued authentic designs, leading to emotional distress, loss of cultural pride, and declining motivation among artisans who feel unseen and economically marginalised.
Section 08

How Might We…?

HMW 1

How might we empower handloom weavers to compete with industrial power looms while preserving culture, creativity, and dignity?

HMW 2

How might we reposition handloom Pochampally Ikat as a premium heritage product and educate urban consumers to recognise and value authentic craftsmanship?

HMW 3

How might we reduce production costs for handloom weavers without compromising quality and craftsmanship?

HMW 4

How might we help handloom weavers earn stable incomes despite cheaper power loom competition — and protect the identity of handloom weaving in a market dominated by imitation?

HMW 5

How might we engage youth and integrate weaving villages into tourism and exports, creating new markets that celebrate authentic Ikat before power loom dominance makes recovery impossible?

Section 09

Reframe Check

Following Wedell-Wedellsborg (HBR, 2017), we challenged our initial framing to uncover richer problem spaces and design opportunities.

Conventional Frame

Mechanisation vs. Tradition: A Zero-Sum Battle

The initial framing treated power looms purely as the villain and handloom weavers purely as victims — a conflict where one must destroy the other.

Reframed Perspective

A Transition in Textile Economies — Adaptation, Not Resistance

Mechanisation represents a shift in consumer expectations and production models. The real challenge is how traditional craftsmanship can adapt and reposition itself through cultural depth, storytelling, and selective innovation — survival through renewal rather than resistance.

Conventional Frame

Power Looms Exploit; Artisans Suffer

From a narrow view, mechanisation is pure exploitation. This framing closes off possibilities for collaboration and shared value creation.

Reframed Perspective

Missing Link: Collaboration Over Competition

From the power loom perspective, the issue is efficiency and accessibility — democratising textiles. The missing link is collaboration: if artisans and technologists co-create, machines can handle scale while artisans preserve authenticity, shifting the narrative from conflict to co-creation with shared value and sustainable livelihoods.

Section 10

Final Problem Statement

The Pochampally handloom weaver faces economic and emotional hardship due to power loom competition that undermines authenticity and devalues craftsmanship.

Empowering them through innovation, protection, and cultural repositioning can ensure that handloom artistry regains market respect, sustains livelihoods, and continues as a living heritage.
Section 11

Meet the Team

Eight students from Mahindra University came together as Idea Igniters — conducting field research, artisan interviews, surveys, and synthesising insights to define this challenge through the lens of human-centred design.
